article_title: Postgres Managed by ClickHouse Enters Public Beta, Free Until Metering Begins June 15

content_preview.title: Postgres Managed by ClickHouse Enters Public Beta, Free Until Metering Begins June 15
content_preview.summary: ClickHouse moved its NVMe-backed managed Postgres service to public beta at Open House 2026, pairing transactional Postgres with native CDC into ClickHouse. It stays free until metering starts June 15.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

ClickHouse, the company behind the analytics database of the same name, has moved its managed transactional Postgres service from private preview into public beta. The service, named "Postgres managed by ClickHouse," runs on local NVMe storage and integrates with ClickHouse through a native change-data-capture (CDC) pipeline. According to the [ClickHouse blog](https://clickhouse.com/blog/postgres-managed-by-clickhouse-beta), the service "remains free until usage metering begins on J...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (877 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear sectioning: Overview, What We Know, The Benchmarks, Business Context, and a What We Don't Know section that explicitly hedges the vendor-run benchmarks. Technical depth (NVMe colocation, native CDC via ClickPipes, pg_clickhouse query pushdown, pgbench/TPC-H methodology) is accurate and appropriately scoped.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all 5 gzipped snapshots from disk (source-0 through source-4); sha256 of each decompressed file matches manifest.json exactly. ALL 5 sources are ClickHouse first-party blog posts (vendor); all returned HTTP 200 with no archive fallback. source-0 (beta announcement): verified verbatim — 'remains free until usage metering begins on June 15, 2026', 'up to 10x faster transactional performance', 'During Beta, all plans include a 50% discount', '$300 in free credits', '1 vCPU / 8 GB RAM / 59 GB NVMe ... starting at approximately $32/month', 'Native CDC via ClickPipes and the pg_clickhouse extension are included at no additional cost', and the GA hedge 'As we move toward General Availability, pricing and packaging may evolve'. source-1 (private preview): verified verbatim — definition quote, NVMe 'physically colocated with compute ... EBS-backed volumes', pg_clickhouse 'comprehensive query pushdown ... FILTERs, JOINs, SEMI-JOINs', 'up to 100X faster analytics', '14 of 22 TPC-H queries ... over 60x', date 'Jan 22, 2026'. source-2 (Open House recap): verified verbatim — 'from private preview to public beta', native CDC pipeline quote, 'over 5x more transactions per second than AWS RDS and 2.4x more than the next closest alternative', TPC-H '54.7 seconds on eight nodes, down from 117.6 seconds on a single node'. source-3 (PostgresBench): methodology verified (pgbench, 256 clients, 16 threads, 'Each run lasts 10 minutes', ~100 GB scale factor); all five per-vendor TPS figures confirmed in the data table at the Large config — ClickHouse 28668, AWS RDS 8133, AWS Aurora 12628, Neon 8563, Crunchy Bridge 14790 — each correctly attributed; 'reproducible benchmark' and 'full benchmark repository is open source' confirmed. source-4 (ARR announcement): Aaron Katz CEO quote verified verbatim, '$250 million in annual run-rate revenue', 'more than 1,000 net new customers since January, bringing its total to 4,000', and 'May 27, 2026' / 'SAN FRANCISCO' Open House dateline confirmed. Every direct quote, every numeric specific, and every date traces to its cited snapshot.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: No hallucinations detected. Every claim maps to a cited source. The article correctly attributes each performance figure to the specific source that published it (the TPS table to PostgresBench, the 5x/2.4x summary to the Open House recap, the analytics 100X/60x figures to the private-preview post). The Databricks Lakebase internal cross-reference (/article/2026-04/12-databricks-lakebase-...) resolves to an existing published article and is not duplicated.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Critically, for a single-vendor product launch the article does not adopt ClickHouse's marketing framing: performance claims are consistently introduced as 'ClickHouse claims', 'ClickHouse says', 'in ClickHouse's own results', and the dedicated 'What We Don't Know' section states plainly that all performance figures are vendor-run and 'have not been independently verified by a third party'. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior article covers 'Postgres managed by ClickHouse'. The only earlier ClickHouse mention (2026-05-22 ClickHouse 26.5 release) concerns the analytics engine, not this managed Postgres OLTP service — no overlap. The Databricks Lakebase piece is a different vendor and is referenced as contrast, not duplicated.
editor_notes.vendor_sourcing_assessment: All five sources are ClickHouse first-party blog posts, which is the central editorial concern for a product-launch announcement. This single-vendor sourcing is acceptable here because (1) the news event is itself a vendor announcement (a public-beta transition), which is legitimately reported from the vendor; (2) every performance claim is explicitly framed and attributed as a vendor-run benchmark rather than stated as fact; and (3) the article includes a prominent 'What We Don't Know' section flagging the absence of independent verification, the configuration-dependence of the numbers, and unresolved durability/failover questions around the NVMe-colocation model. No performance figure is presented as an established fact.
concerns: All five sources are ClickHouse first-party (vendor) blog posts; there is no independent third-party verification of the performance benchmarks. This is correctly disclosed in the article's 'What We Don't Know' section and every figure is attributed to ClickHouse, so it does not rise to a correction, but readers should treat the TPS/TPC-H numbers as vendor claims.
recommendations: If/when a third party independently benchmarks the service or ClickHouse announces a GA date and permanent price list, a follow-up that adds non-vendor sourcing would strengthen the coverage.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, accurately sourced News submission. Despite single-vendor sourcing, the article maintains neutrality through consistent attribution and an explicit limitations section, and every quote, number, and date verifies verbatim against the committed snapshots. Integrity checks pass (signature and hash valid). APPROVE.
